name: Corner quoin
description: Dressed corner reinforcement built from an alternating long-short pattern of large, smooth ashlar-style blocks running up the full height of a building's edge to the eave line. Unlike the rough, uniformly stacked cobble of a rustic stacked-stone-corner, a quoin is finer and deliberately formal: courses alternate between a taller stretcher unit and a shorter header unit so the corner reads as articulated masonry rather than a simple pier. The quoin contrasts the main wall material — typically smooth stone or smooth sandstone against brick, plaster, or coarser stone — so the four corners frame the facade as crisp vertical accents. Applied to all four corners of a rectangular footprint, it gives the volume the classical, ordered character of Renaissance palazzi, Victorian townhouses, and Gothic civic buildings where quoining signals status, permanence, and craftsmanship.
